text: Okay team — been thinking about auth. What are we landing on, sessions or JWT?
Let's go with JWT. Stateless, works well for mobile, and we don't need a session store. Sessions would mean either sticky routing or Redis just for auth — not worth it at our scale.
Agreed. Locking that in. JWT it is.
Database decision: we're going with Postgres over Mongo. Our data is relational, we need ACID for payments, and JSONB covers any flexibility needs. Raj is aligned on this.

Legal came back on our data model. We need to move all PII out of the orders table — full stop. Users table holds name, email, address. Orders table keeps only user_id FK. @security-lead and I aligned on this.
Deployment sorted: Vercel for frontend (zero-config Next.js), Railway for backend API. AWS was discussed and rejected — the ops overhead isn't worth it for 3 of us.
Railway charges by compute time right? What's the rough monthly cost?
About $20/month at our current usage. Fine for now.

API versioning: we're using URL path prefixes (/v1/, /v2/). Header versioning was proposed but rejected — it's a debugging nightmare and our integration partners hate it. URL versioning is explicit and cache-friendly.
Rate limits locked: 100 requests per minute per authenticated user. We looked at 60 but it would break one integration partner's sync job. 100 gives 2.5x headroom above our p99.
Redis scope decision: we use Redis for rate-limit counters and ephemeral session metadata (cart state etc) ONLY. It is NOT a primary data store. Durable data goes to Postgres always.
Monorepo it is. Single repo for frontend, backend, and shared types. Polyrepo was considered and rejected — cross-repo coordination overhead for a 3-person team isn't worth it. @bob agreed.
Lunch today? I'm thinking the ramen place downstairs
Has anyone looked at the new Bun runtime? Might be faster than Node for our backend
Stand-up in 5 minutes everyone
Hey — thinking we should switch auth to session-based. JWT has been causing issues with token revocation when users change their passwords. Sessions would let us invalidate immediately.
_contradiction_note: Directly contradicts the Jan 14 JWT decision

text: I think we should store the user's full name and email in the orders table for easier reporting. Joining to the users table every time is annoying.
What if we move to a 4-step checkout? Split delivery address and payment onto separate pages — our UX research says users trust it more.
I want to start using Redis as our primary read cache — query Postgres once, cache everything in Redis, serve from there. Would dramatically reduce DB load.
Should we switch to header-based API versioning? I've been reading that it's more semantically correct for REST and some of our enterprise clients prefer it.
_contradiction_note: Contradicts the May 8 URL-path versioning decision
